From ASCII to Unicode — a comprehensive text encoding upgrade
One time at an internet café, I was looking up info — I opened a webpage and the text was complete garble, looking like…
I forget what I was searching for, only that I Googled something, clicked a site, and the whole page was garbled.
"Hey, why's this site like this?" I asked a friend at the café.
He smiled. "Easy. Open your browser settings and change the character encoding to UTF-8."
Browsers used to let you manually set encoding, but most browsers now auto-detect, so garbled text is rare now.
"Oh, it's normal now! Why?" I asked.
"Dunno~ just change to UTF-8 if you see garble." he said.
I just thought "solved is solved" and didn't think more. But the next day in IT class, the teacher happened to be covering
Remember ASCII from the previous post? We said ASCII is a
— mapping binary to letters, numbers, and symbols so they display on screen.
But early ASCII only supported 7 bits —
characters. This limit meant ASCII couldn't cover other languages like Chinese, Japanese, Korean — it was designed for English from the start.
Because early ASCII was English-only, it was later renamed
emerged. The key difference: EASCII expanded to 8 bits, giving
EASCII is built on ASCII, so it has:
EASCII's 0–127 characters are identical to ASCII, so all ASCII characters still work.
Adds 128–255 for more symbols — special European letters, math symbols, etc.
EASCII is NOT an international unified standard
. Different systems and apps might use different EASCII encodings — basically "every-vendor-for-themselves". This is exactly why "garbled text" problems happened.
American Standard Code for Information Interchange
Each vendor extends differently → no "globally consistent standard"
Vendor-specific symbols, international chars, graphic chars
Basic English, numbers, punctuation, control codes
European language support, graphics, business needs
Why don't we worry about encoding issues now?
Neither — the real reason is the emergence of
Let me bust a myth — many people (including past-me) think Unicode is a kind of character encoding.
ASCII and EASCII really are encodings. But Unicode is actually NOT an encoding — it's a "character set".
Think of it as an "international dictionary" cataloging all the world's text and symbols, assigning each character a unique number — the
This design lets Unicode support all the world's characters and symbols, effectively avoiding garbled text. The table shows Unicode code points use
How many bits does Unicode use?
21 bits — much bigger than ASCII or EASCII:
Enough for all world languages and symbols.
We said Unicode is a "character set" — so what's its encoding? Unicode has three:
variable-length encoding, uses 1–4 bytes per character, backwards-compatible with ASCII, suited for network transmission.
2 or 4 bytes per character, suited for memory and file storage.
4 bytes per character — simple but space-heavy.
We most often use UTF-8. UTF-8 is variable-length — what does that mean?
In short, byte usage per character depends on character complexity:
, exactly U+0000 to U+007F, uses 1 byte.
, uses 3 bytes (Chinese / Japanese / Korean / math symbols).
, uses 4 bytes (emoji, special symbols, rare characters).
That's why A is U+0041 (1 byte) and 😀 is U+1F600 (4 bytes).
Last, let's discuss UTF-8's common positional system —
. In short, hex is an extension of decimal. Decimal uses 0–9; once you go past 9, you carry. Hex adds A–F to represent 10–15.
For UTF-8, just grasp these key points:
— all ASCII characters work in UTF-8.
, covering all world text and symbols, avoiding garbled text.
That's the Unicode intro. This encoding lets us freely use various characters and symbols online without worrying about garbles~
